```markdown
---
name: sales-revenue-ops-discovery-call-prep-stakeholder-brief
description: >
  Prepare stakeholder communication for discovery call prep: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "discovery call prep stakeholder-brief", /sales-revenue-ops-discovery-call-prep-stakeholder-brief.
metadata:
  category: sales
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /sales-revenue-ops-discovery-call-prep-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for discovery call prep: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "discovery call prep stakeholder-brief", /sales-revenue-ops-discovery-call-prep-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when colleagues are joining a discovery call and need to arrive as one coordinated team: an exec sitting in on a strategic account, an SE covering technical threads, a manager shadowing, or a CS lead on an expansion call. The brief exists to prevent the classic multi-attendee failure - three people from your side each running their own meeting, the exec answering questions the rep wanted the prospect to answer, and nobody landing the next-step ask.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Who's joining from your side, why they were invited, and their familiarity with the account (often near zero)
- The account context: who booked the call, the trigger, prospect attendees with roles, deal history if any
- The call plan: ranked problem hypotheses, the question ladder, the scripted next-step ask
- Each internal attendee's specific job on the call - and what they should not do
- Logistics: date/time, duration, platform, who sends the agenda to the prospect

## Workflow

1. Open with the one-paragraph account picture a busy colleague will actually read: who the prospect is, the trigger that produced this meeting, who's attending from their side and what each attendee likely wants, and what the deal could be. Write it for a smart person with zero context - the exec skimming this in the hallway before joining.
2. State the call's single objective and its exit condition: which hypothesis must be tested, and the specific next step the team will ask for before the call ends (named attendees, proposed date). Everyone on the call should be able to recite both; put them in a box at the top.
3. Assign roles with boundaries, not just duties: the rep opens, runs the ladder, and owns the close - including the next-step ask; the SE takes the technical thread when the rep hands it over and hands it back; the exec builds peer rapport and asks one or two strategic questions max. Spell out the anti-jobs explicitly: the exec does not pitch, nobody answers a question the prospect should answer, nobody promises roadmap or pricing.
4. Share the question ladder with attribution - who asks what - so seniors don't burn the rep's best questions as small talk. Mark the impact-quantification question and who owns it; it's the one most often lost when a call gets crowded.
5. Include the landmines: topics to avoid (a live support issue, a past failed evaluation, an unverified rumor about their org), the competitor if known (and the rule: no disparagement), and anything promised to the prospect already. A guest attendee who steps on a landmine undoes months of rep work in one sentence.
6. Close with logistics and the pre-call sync: a 10-minute huddle before the call to confirm roles, plus who captures notes and who drafts the follow-up - which goes to the rep for approval before anything is sent to the prospect. Deliver the brief to the user for their review and internal distribution.

## Output format

**Objective** - the call, date, and the single exit condition in a box

**Account picture** - one paragraph, zero-context readable

**Their side / our side**

| Attendee | Role | What they want / their job | What they must not do |

**Question ladder with attribution** - who asks what, money question flagged

**Landmines** - topics to avoid, competitor rule, prior promises

**Logistics & sync** - huddle time, note owner, follow-up drafter (rep approves before send)

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Write the account picture for a reader with zero prior context
- Give every internal attendee both a job and an anti-job
- Attribute the questions - especially the impact question - to a named asker
- Schedule the 10-minute pre-call huddle as part of the brief
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't brief guests with a deck's worth of context they won't read - one page, hierarchy of importance
- Don't let seniority decide who closes; the rep owns the next-step ask regardless of who's in the room
- Don't include unverified account gossip as fact; label rumor as rumor or leave it out
- Don't send the follow-up or agenda to the prospect from this brief - drafts go to the rep for approval, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The objective and exit condition are stated once, at the top, recitable
- [ ] Every internal attendee has a job, an anti-job, and attributed questions
- [ ] Landmines and the no-disparagement rule are explicit
- [ ] The pre-call huddle, note owner, and follow-up approval path are named
- [ ] All prospect-facing items are drafts behind rep approval; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The objection verbatim, from real calls or emails - the actual words, not the category
- Where in the cycle it appears (first call? post-proposal? at legal?) and from which persona
- What happens next in deals where it appears: won anyway, stalled, lost - with a few concrete examples
- What reps currently say in response, and whether anything has worked repeatably
- Constraints on responses: pricing floors, roadmap commitments that cannot be made, legal language limits

## Workflow

1. Diagnose before strategizing: is this objection real, a smokescreen, or a symptom? "Too expensive" after a proposal with no quantified impact is usually a value-communication failure upstream, not a pricing problem. Look at where it appears in the cycle and what the buyer does next. The memo's first finding is what the objection actually is - because the right response to a symptom is fixing the upstream stage, not a better rebuttal.
2. Establish the isolation test as step zero of any response: "If we resolved [objection], would you move forward?" A yes makes it worth solving; a hedge reveals the real objection behind it. Any recommended strategy that skips isolation treats every stated objection as true, and buyers often lead with the socially easiest one rather than the real one.
3. Lay out the strategic options with tradeoffs: (a) reframe - anchor against the cost of the problem (using the buyer's own numbers from discovery); requires that discovery actually captured numbers; (b) restructure - options/tiers, phasing, term changes; preserves price integrity but adds complexity and can look like flinching if offered too fast; (c) confront with proof - reference calls, pilots, guarantees where approved; strongest when the objection is factual doubt, expensive to fulfill; (d) disqualify earlier - if deals with this objection almost never close, the cheapest fix is surfacing it in call one and walking sooner.
4. Ground each option in the deal history gathered: which responses have preceded actual wins (not just pleasant conversations)? Where history is thin, mark the recommendation as a hypothesis with a review date - do not decorate it with invented conversion claims.
5. Recommend one strategy and script it in the acknowledge → isolate → respond → confirm shape, in speakable words, with the buyer's own discovery numbers slotted in where the reframe needs them. Include the guardrails: what reps may not do in response (unapproved discounts, roadmap promises, legal-term concessions) and who approves exceptions.
6. Define what feeds back: reps log the objection verbatim and the outcome, so the strategy gets re-graded on evidence at the review date. Note that if the objection traces to product or pricing reality, the memo should say so plainly and route that finding to the owner who can fix it - a talk track cannot patch a real gap forever.

## Output format

**Objective** - the objection (verbatim), where it appears, and the decision

**Diagnosis** - real / smokescreen / symptom, with the evidence from deal history

**Options** - reframe / restructure / confront / disqualify-earlier, each with tradeoffs and evidence

**Recommendation** - chosen strategy, scripted in acknowledge → isolate → respond → confirm form

**Guardrails** - what reps may not concede, and the exception approver

**Feedback & review** - what gets logged, the review date, and the re-grade condition

**Upstream finding** (if any) - the product/pricing/discovery gap this objection actually signals, routed to its owner

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Work from the objection verbatim - categories hide the actual concern
- Put the isolation test at the front of any scripted response
- Anchor reframes in numbers the buyer gave during discovery
- Say plainly when the objection is telling the company something true
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't script rebuttals that "overcome" a legitimate concern by outtalking it - dismissed concerns return at contract time with reinforcements
- Don't reach for discount as the default answer to price objections; it teaches the market to object
- Don't let scripts promise features, dates, or legal terms reps can't guarantee
- Don't fabricate win-rate evidence for a preferred strategy, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The diagnosis (real/smokescreen/symptom) is stated with deal-history evidence
- [ ] The recommended script opens with the isolation test and stays speakable
- [ ] Guardrails name what cannot be conceded and who approves exceptions
- [ ] Evidence gaps are marked as hypotheses with a review date - no invented statistics
- [ ] Upstream product/pricing findings are routed, not buried; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-decision-memo or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for objection handling: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "objection handling operating-plan", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: sales
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for objection handling: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "objection handling operating-plan", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants a living objection playbook instead of tribal knowledge: real objections collected from calls, categorized and diagnosed, responses drafted and pressure-tested by the people who face them, reps drilled through role-play, and the whole thing kept current as pricing, product, and competitors move. A playbook written once by enablement and never touched again is shelf-ware within two quarters.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Raw material available: call recordings, CRM loss reasons, rep memory (in that order of reliability)
- Team shape: how many reps, experience spread, who the strongest objection-handlers are
- Existing assets: any current battlecards or scripts, and whether anyone uses them
- Constraints that bound responses: discount authority, roadmap commitment rules, legal language limits
- Where the playbook will live so reps actually reach it mid-deal, and who owns keeping it alive

## Workflow

1. Collect objections from evidence, not memory alone: pull the actual phrasings from call recordings and lost-deal notes over a defined window. Capture verbatim wording, persona, cycle stage, and what happened to the deal. Rep recollection rounds everything to "pricing" - recordings preserve the objection the buyer actually raised, which is what the response must fit.
2. Categorize and diagnose: group into price/value, status quo/incumbent, timing/priority, authority/process, product gap, and trust/risk. For each cluster, mark whether it's typically real, a smokescreen for something else, or a symptom of an upstream failure (weak discovery, wrong persona). The diagnosis decides whether the playbook entry is a response script or a process fix - write both kinds.
3. Draft entries in a fixed format per objection: the objection verbatim (top 2-3 phrasings), the isolation question first ("if we solved this, what else would stand in the way?"), the acknowledge-explore-respond-confirm flow in speakable language, the proof asset that backs it (reference, data the company can actually substantiate, demo path), and the hard guardrail - what a rep may not concede (discounts beyond authority, roadmap dates, legal terms) and who to route exceptions to.
4. Pressure-test drafts with the best objection-handlers before publishing: a working session where top reps red-line the scripts against how buyers actually push back. Scripts written by enablement alone read like brochures under pressure; the field edit is what makes them survivable.
5. Drill, don't distribute: schedule role-play blocks where reps face the objections cold and managers score against the entry (did they isolate first? did they stay out of guardrail territory?). One drill cycle at launch, then a recurring slot in team meetings for the top objections of the month. Reading a playbook is not a skill; recovering under pressure is.
6. Wire the feedback loop: reps log new or shifted objections (verbatim, persona, stage) in a defined place; the playbook owner reviews monthly, updates entries, and retires stale ones - especially after any pricing change, competitor move, or product release, each of which invalidates specific entries on a predictable schedule.
7. Route the systemic findings: objection clusters that trace to product gaps or pricing structure go to the owners who can change those things, with the evidence attached. Assign every workstream - collection, drafting, field review, drills, maintenance - an owner and a date in the plan table.

## Output format

**Objective** - the playbook's scope, team, and launch date

**Collection window** - sources, timeframe, capture fields (verbatim, persona, stage, outcome)

**Category map**

| Cluster | Typical diagnosis | Response type (script / process fix) | Frequency in sample |

**Entry template** - verbatim phrasings, isolation question, response flow, proof asset, guardrail + exception route

**Operating plan**

| Workstream | Owner | Deadline | Done means | Risk |

**Drill schedule** - launch cycle, recurring slot, scoring criteria

**Maintenance** - feedback log location, review cadence, invalidation triggers (pricing/competitor/product changes)

**Systemic findings route** - cluster → owner who can fix the underlying issue

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Source objections verbatim from recordings and loss notes before rep memory
- Open every entry with the isolation question
- Put the guardrail (and exception approver) inside each entry, not in an appendix
- Let the best field reps red-line every script before it ships
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't write "overcomes" that steamroll legitimate concerns - entries should distinguish concerns to resolve from signals to disqualify
- Don't include claims or statistics in proof assets the company cannot substantiate
- Don't let the playbook promise features, dates, or terms reps lack authority to give
- Don't ship without the drill cycle - an unread playbook is a report, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Objections are captured verbatim with persona, stage, and outcome
- [ ] Every entry has isolation first, a speakable flow, a substantiated proof asset, and a guardrail
- [ ] Field-rep review and drill cycles are scheduled workstreams with owners
- [ ] Maintenance triggers (pricing, competitor, product changes) name the update owner
- [ ] Systemic findings route to owners who can fix root causes; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

Universal path: ./skills/sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review/SKILL.md
Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review/SKILL.md

```markdown
---
name: sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review
description: >
  Review operational risk for objection handling: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "objection handling risk-review", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: sales
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review

Review operational risk for objection handling: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "objection handling risk-review", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to audit how a team actually handles objections - the playbook, the habits, and the incentives around them - for the failure modes that cost deals and post-sale trust: the discount reflex, rehearsed rebuttals that dismiss real concerns, overpromising to get past a hard question, arguing with prospects, and smokescreen objections taken at face value while the real blocker walks the deal out the back door.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current playbook or scripts, if any, and whether reps actually use them
- Evidence of practice: call recordings or manager shadowing notes showing objections handled live
- Discount and concession data: how often price objections end in discounts, at what depth, approved by whom
- Post-sale signals: churn or escalations traceable to sales-stage promises; what implementation/CS hears customers say they were told
- Loss reasons logged against objection-heavy deals over a recent window

## Workflow

1. Audit the discount reflex with numbers: of deals where a price objection was logged, how often was the response a discount, how deep, and how fast after the objection? A pattern of same-call discounts means the team is answering fear, not the objection - and training buyers (and reps) that objecting is how you get 15% off. Mitigation lives in authority design and drilled alternatives, not memos urging discipline.
2. Audit for overpromise risk - the objection-handling failure that detonates after signature: sample calls and proposals for feature commitments, dates, or legal-adjacent assurances given to defuse objections. Cross-check against what implementation/CS later hears ("sales told us it does X"). Every confirmed instance is a high-impact row; the mitigation is a written no-promise list per topic (roadmap, terms, custom work) with a named exception approver.
3. Audit for dismissal and argument patterns: rehearsed comebacks that outtalk the concern ("actually, our customers find the opposite"), interrupting the objection midway, or debating factual points with the prospect. Dismissed concerns don't disappear; they return at procurement with reinforcements. Check whether scripts and drills open with acknowledgment and the isolation question, or jump straight to rebuttal.
4. Audit the smokescreen gap: do reps isolate ("if we solved this, would you move forward?") before responding? Review stalled deals where the logged objection was "resolved" yet the deal died anyway - the signature of answering the stated objection while the real one (authority, competing project, no budget owner) went unasked.
5. Audit the listening infrastructure: are objections logged verbatim anywhere, or does the org only learn about them in loss reviews? A team that can't see its objection distribution can't fix upstream causes - and recurring objection clusters that trace to product or pricing reality need routing to those owners, not better talk tracks forever.
6. Compile the risk table - likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner - separating behavioral risks (fixed by drills, script changes, authority design) from structural ones (fixed by product, pricing, or discovery-stage changes). Mark act-now items: any live overpromising pattern, and any discount authority with no approval gate.

## Output format

**Objective** - the team/playbook under review and the evidence window

**Inputs used**

**Risk table**

| Risk | Evidence found | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Owner |

**Discount-reflex audit** - frequency, depth, speed-to-discount, approval path status

**Overpromise audit** - instances found, post-sale echoes, no-promise list status

**Dismissal & smokescreen audit** - script/drill patterns, isolation-question usage, stalled-deal signature

**Structural findings** - objection clusters pointing at product/pricing/discovery, routed to owners

**Act-now items** - with owner and date

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Ground every finding in sampled calls, proposals, or logged data - not folklore
- Treat confirmed overpromising as the highest-impact row in the table
- Check for the isolation question as the hinge behavior in scripts and drills
- Separate behavioral fixes from structural ones and route each to its real owner
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't frame the review as rep-blaming - audit the incentives and authority design that produce the behavior
- Don't accept "we rarely discount" without pulling the concession data
- Don't let a talk-track fix stand in for a product or pricing finding the evidence supports
- Don't contact prospects or customers to verify claims without explicit approval, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every risk row cites the evidence sample behind it
- [ ] Discount reflex is quantified (frequency, depth, speed) rather than asserted
- [ ] Overpromise findings include the post-sale cross-check and a no-promise-list mitigation
- [ ] Behavioral and structural risks are separated with distinct owners
- [ ] Act-now items are dated; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

---

# /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review

Review operational risk for objection handling: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "objection handling risk-review", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to audit how a team actually handles objections - the playbook, the habits, and the incentives around them - for the failure modes that cost deals and post-sale trust: the discount reflex, rehearsed rebuttals that dismiss real concerns, overpromising to get past a hard question, arguing with prospects, and smokescreen objections taken at face value while the real blocker walks the deal out the back door.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current playbook or scripts, if any, and whether reps actually use them
- Evidence of practice: call recordings or manager shadowing notes showing objections handled live
- Discount and concession data: how often price objections end in discounts, at what depth, approved by whom
- Post-sale signals: churn or escalations traceable to sales-stage promises; what implementation/CS hears customers say they were told
- Loss reasons logged against objection-heavy deals over a recent window

## Workflow

1. Audit the discount reflex with numbers: of deals where a price objection was logged, how often was the response a discount, how deep, and how fast after the objection? A pattern of same-call discounts means the team is answering fear, not the objection - and training buyers (and reps) that objecting is how you get 15% off. Mitigation lives in authority design and drilled alternatives, not memos urging discipline.
2. Audit for overpromise risk - the objection-handling failure that detonates after signature: sample calls and proposals for feature commitments, dates, or legal-adjacent assurances given to defuse objections. Cross-check against what implementation/CS later hears ("sales told us it does X"). Every confirmed instance is a high-impact row; the mitigation is a written no-promise list per topic (roadmap, terms, custom work) with a named exception approver.
3. Audit for dismissal and argument patterns: rehearsed comebacks that outtalk the concern ("actually, our customers find the opposite"), interrupting the objection midway, or debating factual points with the prospect. Dismissed concerns don't disappear; they return at procurement with reinforcements. Check whether scripts and drills open with acknowledgment and the isolation question, or jump straight to rebuttal.
4. Audit the smokescreen gap: do reps isolate ("if we solved this, would you move forward?") before responding? Review stalled deals where the logged objection was "resolved" yet the deal died anyway - the signature of answering the stated objection while the real one (authority, competing project, no budget owner) went unasked.
5. Audit the listening infrastructure: are objections logged verbatim anywhere, or does the org only learn about them in loss reviews? A team that can't see its objection distribution can't fix upstream causes - and recurring objection clusters that trace to product or pricing reality need routing to those owners, not better talk tracks forever.
6. Compile the risk table - likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner - separating behavioral risks (fixed by drills, script changes, authority design) from structural ones (fixed by product, pricing, or discovery-stage changes). Mark act-now items: any live overpromising pattern, and any discount authority with no approval gate.

## Output format

**Objective** - the team/playbook under review and the evidence window

**Inputs used**

**Risk table**

| Risk | Evidence found | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Owner |

**Discount-reflex audit** - frequency, depth, speed-to-discount, approval path status

**Overpromise audit** - instances found, post-sale echoes, no-promise list status

**Dismissal & smokescreen audit** - script/drill patterns, isolation-question usage, stalled-deal signature

**Structural findings** - objection clusters pointing at product/pricing/discovery, routed to owners

**Act-now items** - with owner and date

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Ground every finding in sampled calls, proposals, or logged data - not folklore
- Treat confirmed overpromising as the highest-impact row in the table
- Check for the isolation question as the hinge behavior in scripts and drills
- Separate behavioral fixes from structural ones and route each to its real owner
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't frame the review as rep-blaming - audit the incentives and authority design that produce the behavior
- Don't accept "we rarely discount" without pulling the concession data
- Don't let a talk-track fix stand in for a product or pricing finding the evidence supports
- Don't contact prospects or customers to verify claims without explicit approval, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every risk row cites the evidence sample behind it
- [ ] Discount reflex is quantified (frequency, depth, speed) rather than asserted
- [ ] Overpromise findings include the post-sale cross-check and a no-promise-list mitigation
- [ ] Behavioral and structural risks are separated with distinct owners
- [ ] Act-now items are dated; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

```markdown
---
name: sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-stakeholder-brief
description: >
  Prepare stakeholder communication for objection handling: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "objection handling stakeholder-brief", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-stakeholder-brief.
metadata:

---
# /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for objection handling: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "objection handling stakeholder-brief", /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when what buyers are objecting to needs to reach the people who can act on it: leadership deciding whether "too expensive" is a packaging problem, product hearing which gaps actually block deals (with evidence, not rep lobbying), or enablement getting the mandate for a playbook and drill program. Objection data is the field's report on the company's story meeting reality - this brief is how that report gets read.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Audience: exec leadership, product, pricing committee, or enablement - each acts on different slices
- The objection data: verbatim phrasings, frequency, persona, cycle stage, and deal outcomes over a defined window
- Source quality: how much comes from recordings/logged fields vs. rep recollection - stated honestly
- Revenue linkage: value of deals lost or stalled where each objection cluster was logged
- The asks: what decision or resource is being requested from this audience

## Workflow

1. Lead with the finding, sized in revenue: "In Q2, [objection cluster] appeared in N logged deals worth $X; those deals closed at a visibly lower rate than the rest of the pipeline." Use your own CRM numbers and state the sample size - a pattern across nine deals is worth reporting, but it's nine deals, and the brief says so.
2. Present the top clusters with verbatim quotes: two or three actual buyer phrasings per cluster, with persona and stage. Verbatim is what makes product and leadership believe it - "buyers say pricing is opaque for multi-region teams" lands where "price objections are up" doesn't. Keep quotes anonymous per source policy.
3. For each cluster, give the field's read on the diagnosis: genuinely priced-out vs. value not landing vs. missing capability vs. smokescreen with a deeper cause - and what evidence supports the read. Where the diagnosis is uncertain, say what would settle it (e.g., win-loss interviews on the affected deals) rather than picking the convenient story.
4. Separate what sales can fix from what it can't, and say both plainly: better isolation and reframing skills, earlier surfacing - sales-side, with the enablement plan attached; a real capability gap or a pricing structure mismatch - product/pricing-side, with the affected-deal evidence attached. A brief that pins everything on rep skill (or everything on product) reads as politics and gets discounted accordingly.
5. Make the asks concrete per audience: a pricing-committee review of one specific structure issue by a date; a product decision (build, don't-build, or give the field an approved answer) on one named gap; budget/mandate for the drill program with its schedule. One owner and date per ask - "product should be aware" is not an ask.
6. Close with what the field will do regardless (playbook updates, drills, tighter logging) and when the next objection report lands, so the brief establishes a rhythm rather than a one-time complaint. Deliver as a draft for the user to review and distribute.

## Output format

**Objective** - the headline finding, revenue-sized, with sample size stated

**Top objection clusters**

| Cluster | Verbatim examples | Persona / stage | Deals affected ($) | Outcome pattern |

**Field diagnosis per cluster** - the read, its evidence, and what would settle uncertainty

**Sales-side actions** - what the field owns, with the enablement plan

**Asks of this audience**

| Ask | Owner | Decision needed by | Evidence attached |

**Data honesty note** - sources (recordings vs. recollection), window, known gaps

**Next report date**

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Size every cluster in your own deal data with the sample size visible
- Use anonymized verbatim quotes - they carry the persuasion
- Split sales-fixable from company-fixable and commit to the sales side
- Ask for decisions with owners and dates, not awareness
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't inflate the pattern with invented percentages or industry benchmark claims
- Don't present rep recollection as logged data - label the source quality
- Don't turn the brief into a product-team indictment; it's evidence for a decision, not a blame memo
- Don't attach customer-identifying quotes without clearance, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The headline finding is revenue-sized from own data with sample size stated
- [ ] Each cluster shows verbatim, anonymized buyer language
- [ ] Sales-side commitments accompany the asks made of others
- [ ] Every ask has an owner and a decision date
- [ ] The brief is a draft for the user to distribute; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sales-revenue-ops-objection-handling-stakeholder-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: sales-revenue-ops-proposal-outline-decision-memo
description: >
  Write a decision memo for proposal outlines: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "proposal outlines decision-memo", /sales-revenue-ops-proposal-outline-decision-memo.
metadata:
  category: sales
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /sales-revenue-ops-proposal-outline-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for proposal outlines: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "proposal outlines decision-memo", /sales-revenue-ops-proposal-outline-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this before drafting a proposal, when the shaping decisions are still open: whether the deal has earned a proposal at all, single option versus a three-tier structure, how to anchor price against quantified impact, what scope goes in versus gets phased, and whether a mutual action plan rides along. A proposal is a decision document for the buyer's internal sale - the memo decides what decision it's engineered to produce.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Discovery evidence: the problem in the buyer's words, quantified impact (their numbers), and who confirmed them
- The buying process as stated: decision makers, evaluation criteria, procurement steps, target date
- Who will read the proposal that the rep has never met, and what those readers care about (CFO: risk and payback; legal: terms)
- Pricing latitude: list, floor, approval chain for anything between
- Competitive context and any deadline pressure on either side

## Workflow

1. Gate first: has this deal earned a proposal? Earned means discovery produced a problem statement in the buyer's own words, an impact number they agreed to, and a named decision process. A proposal sent to "see if there's interest" is a brochure with pricing - it surrenders anchoring, invites silent comparison shopping, and can't be unsent. If the gate fails, the memo's recommendation is the discovery work still owed, and it says so plainly.
2. Decide the structure against the buying reality: single option (clean when the solution is confirmed and the champion wants a rubber stamp), or three tiers (useful when scope appetite is genuinely uncertain - anchors high, gives procurement somewhere to "win," and keeps the middle option the honest recommendation). Tiers must be real alternatives the company would happily deliver; a decoy tier the team can't staff is a trap for everyone.
3. Decide the anchoring: the proposal restates the quantified problem before any price appears, so cost lands against the impact number the buyer already owns. If discovery never produced that number, price will anchor against zero - which is another way of saying the gate in step 1 failed.
4. Decide scope boundaries explicitly: what's in, what's phased to later, what's excluded and priced separately if asked. Ambiguous scope reads as generosity and returns as margin erosion and a resentful delivery team. The memo names the scope line and who confirmed deliverability.
5. Decide the close mechanics that ride with the proposal: a mutual action plan - dated steps from proposal delivery to signature, each with a named owner on both sides (legal review, security questionnaire, signer availability) - and an expiration or review date on pricing so the proposal can't float unsigned for a quarter. A proposal without a path is a PDF waiting to be ghosted.
6. Weigh the tradeoffs and recommend: structure, anchor, scope line, and action plan, with the reasoning a deal desk could audit. List what would change the recommendation (new stakeholder appears, competitor moves, scope balloons).
7. State the send gate: the outline goes to drafting, and the drafted proposal is reviewed by the rep and required approvers (pricing/deal desk where applicable) before anything reaches the buyer. This memo authorizes drafting only.

## Output format

**Objective** - the deal, its stage, and the proposal decision at hand

**Earned-proposal gate** - the discovery evidence, item by item; verdict (proceed / discovery owed)

**Structure decision** - single vs. tiers, with the buying-reality reasoning

**Anchor** - the quantified impact (buyer's number, who confirmed it) the price will sit against

**Scope line** - in / phased / excluded, deliverability confirmed by whom

**Close mechanics** - mutual action plan skeleton (dated, owner-per-step, both sides) and pricing expiration

**Recommendation & reversal conditions**

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action** - drafting begins; buyer sees nothing before rep + approver sign-off

## Do

- Refuse the proposal politely when discovery hasn't earned it - and say what would
- Make every tier deliverable and honestly priced, with the recommendation visible
- Put the buyer's own impact number ahead of any price
- Attach a dated, owner-per-step mutual action plan through signature
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't send a proposal as a discovery substitute - it converts curiosity into ghosting
- Don't build decoy tiers the team couldn't deliver if chosen
- Don't leave pricing valid indefinitely; undated proposals stall deals through quarter-end
- Don't let anything reach the buyer without rep and approver review, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The earned-proposal gate ran on discovery evidence, and its verdict drives everything after
- [ ] The structure choice cites the buying reality, not habit
- [ ] Price anchors against a buyer-confirmed impact number (or the gate failed)
- [ ] The mutual action plan has dates and owners on both sides, through signature
- [ ] The memo authorizes drafting only; sending sits behind rep + approver gates, and any connector or high-stakes action requires sign-off
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sales-revenue-ops-proposal-outline-decision-memo or describe the task from the skill description.

Review operational risk for proposal outlines: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "proposal outlines risk-review", /sales-revenue-ops-proposal-outline-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before a proposal goes out, or after one has gone quiet - a structured check on what could sink this specific deal, so the rep addresses it instead of just hoping.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Deal size relative to the buyer's normal spend (a proposal that's unusually large for them carries different risk than a routine renewal-sized ask)
- Who has and hasn't been in the room - procurement, legal, security review, budget owner
- Timeline pressure and whether it's real (a fiscal deadline) or soft (a rep-created deadline)

## Workflow

1. Check for missing stakeholders first - a proposal that hasn't touched procurement/legal/security yet for a deal that will need them is the single most common cause of late-stage stalls.
2. Assess pricing risk: is this priced against a real budget number the prospect confirmed, or a guess? Unconfirmed budget is a top reason proposals go quiet.
3. Check competitive exposure: is a competitor proposal also on the table, and does this one differentiate on anything beyond price?
4. Check the paper trail: are terms (payment schedule, contract length, cancellation terms) likely to trigger legal review delays given what's known about this buyer's procurement process?
5. Rate each risk by likelihood and impact honestly - a rep's job is to surface the scary risk, not bury it under optimistic framing.
6. For each real risk, assign a specific next action and owner, not a vague "monitor."

**Deal snapshot** - size, stage, days since proposal sent

**Risk table**

| Unconfirmed budget | | | | |

| Contract/legal friction | | | | |

4. Name the one or two likely objections from someone in this reader's role and address them directly rather than waiting to be asked.
5. End with a single, specific ask and a date.

  Write a decision memo for CRM hygiene: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "CRM hygiene decision-memo", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-decision-memo.

# /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for CRM hygiene: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "CRM hygiene decision-memo", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a RevOps or sales leader knows the CRM is dirty and must choose where to start: purge zombie deals, tighten stage definitions, enforce required fields, dedupe accounts, or fix ownership rules. These compete for the same political capital and rep attention - sequencing them wrong (e.g., adding required fields before stages mean anything) produces compliance theater instead of trustworthy pipeline data.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The symptom that triggered this: forecast misses, duplicate outreach embarrassments, reports nobody trusts, an audit, a new leader asking why pipeline is 5x quota
- Current state evidence: count of open deals with no activity in 60+ days, deals with past-due close dates, stage definitions (written anywhere?), duplicate rate if known
- Who feels the pain (leadership? reps? marketing attribution?) and who would bear the cleanup work
- CRM platform, existing validation rules, and automations that touch opportunity or contact records
- Appetite: is leadership willing to see the pipeline number drop when fiction is removed?

## Workflow

1. Trace the symptom to its mechanism before picking a fix. Forecast misses usually trace to stage definitions that mean nothing ("Negotiation" containing deals that never had a meeting) and zombie deals inflating coverage; duplicate outreach traces to dupes and ownership rules. The memo's decision is which mechanism to fix first - name it from evidence, not preference.
2. Quantify the dirt cheaply: open deals with no activity 60+ days (the zombie count and their summed value), deals with close dates in the past, opportunities missing amount or next step, obvious duplicate accounts. These few queries turn "the CRM is a mess" into a decision-grade baseline.
3. Frame 2-3 sequencing options with real tradeoffs. Typical: (a) zombie purge first - fast, makes pipeline honest, but the number visibly drops and reps feel policed; (b) stage-exit criteria first - fixes the forecast's foundation, slower, requires manager enforcement in pipeline reviews; (c) field/dedupe enforcement first - helps attribution and outreach, but tightening fields on top of meaningless stages hardens bad data.
4. State the political cost honestly in each option: required fields add friction reps will route around (junk values like "TBD" and 1/1/2099 dates) unless managers inspect; a purge without an archive step reads as deleting reps' work. A hygiene program that ignores rep incentives fails in a quarter.
5. Recommend one starting move plus the enforcement mechanism that keeps it fixed - hygiene decays without a standing inspection point (stage criteria checked at pipeline review; a weekly zombie report to managers). A cleanup without a mechanism is scheduled to be repeated.
6. Define the safety rails as conditions of proceeding: full export/backup before any bulk change, archive-don't-delete for purged deals, a dry-run list reviewed by sales managers before anything is mass-updated, and rep notice with a grace window to rescue real deals.
7. Set the success measure in data terms (e.g., zero open deals past close date, zombie count near zero and staying there for two consecutive months) and the review date. Note explicitly what the pipeline number is expected to do - shrink - and why that's the point.

## Output format

**Objective** - the symptom, its traced mechanism, and the decision

**Baseline evidence** - zombie count/value, past-due close dates, missing-field counts, dupe estimate

**Options** - 2-3 sequencing choices with operational and political tradeoffs

**Recommendation** - the starting move, its enforcement mechanism, and why it beats the alternatives

**Safety rails** - backup, archive-not-delete, manager-reviewed dry run, rep grace window

**Success measure** - the data condition, the review date, and the expected (smaller) pipeline number

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Trace symptoms to mechanisms before recommending a fix
- Quantify the mess with a handful of cheap queries first
- Pair every cleanup with the standing enforcement mechanism that prevents recurrence

  Write a decision memo for CRM hygiene: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "CRM hygiene decision-memo", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-decision-memo.

# /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for CRM hygiene: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "CRM hygiene decision-memo", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a RevOps or sales leader knows the CRM is dirty and must choose where to start: purge zombie deals, tighten stage definitions, enforce required fields, dedupe accounts, or fix ownership rules. These compete for the same political capital and rep attention - sequencing them wrong (e.g., adding required fields before stages mean anything) produces compliance theater instead of trustworthy pipeline data.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The symptom that triggered this: forecast misses, duplicate outreach embarrassments, reports nobody trusts, an audit, a new leader asking why pipeline is 5x quota
- Current state evidence: count of open deals with no activity in 60+ days, deals with past-due close dates, stage definitions (written anywhere?), duplicate rate if known
- Who feels the pain (leadership? reps? marketing attribution?) and who would bear the cleanup work
- CRM platform, existing validation rules, and automations that touch opportunity or contact records
- Appetite: is leadership willing to see the pipeline number drop when fiction is removed?

## Workflow

1. Trace the symptom to its mechanism before picking a fix. Forecast misses usually trace to stage definitions that mean nothing ("Negotiation" containing deals that never had a meeting) and zombie deals inflating coverage; duplicate outreach traces to dupes and ownership rules. The memo's decision is which mechanism to fix first - name it from evidence, not preference.
2. Quantify the dirt cheaply: open deals with no activity 60+ days (the zombie count and their summed value), deals with close dates in the past, opportunities missing amount or next step, obvious duplicate accounts. These few queries turn "the CRM is a mess" into a decision-grade baseline.
3. Frame 2-3 sequencing options with real tradeoffs. Typical: (a) zombie purge first - fast, makes pipeline honest, but the number visibly drops and reps feel policed; (b) stage-exit criteria first - fixes the forecast's foundation, slower, requires manager enforcement in pipeline reviews; (c) field/dedupe enforcement first - helps attribution and outreach, but tightening fields on top of meaningless stages hardens bad data.
4. State the political cost honestly in each option: required fields add friction reps will route around (junk values like "TBD" and 1/1/2099 dates) unless managers inspect; a purge without an archive step reads as deleting reps' work. A hygiene program that ignores rep incentives fails in a quarter.
5. Recommend one starting move plus the enforcement mechanism that keeps it fixed - hygiene decays without a standing inspection point (stage criteria checked at pipeline review; a weekly zombie report to managers). A cleanup without a mechanism is scheduled to be repeated.
6. Define the safety rails as conditions of proceeding: full export/backup before any bulk change, archive-don't-delete for purged deals, a dry-run list reviewed by sales managers before anything is mass-updated, and rep notice with a grace window to rescue real deals.
7. Set the success measure in data terms (e.g., zero open deals past close date, zombie count near zero and staying there for two consecutive months) and the review date. Note explicitly what the pipeline number is expected to do - shrink - and why that's the point.

## Output format

**Objective** - the symptom, its traced mechanism, and the decision

**Baseline evidence** - zombie count/value, past-due close dates, missing-field counts, dupe estimate

**Options** - 2-3 sequencing choices with operational and political tradeoffs

**Recommendation** - the starting move, its enforcement mechanism, and why it beats the alternatives

**Safety rails** - backup, archive-not-delete, manager-reviewed dry run, rep grace window

**Success measure** - the data condition, the review date, and the expected (smaller) pipeline number

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Trace symptoms to mechanisms before recommending a fix
- Quantify the mess with a handful of cheap queries first
- Pair every cleanup with the standing enforcement mechanism that prevents recurrence
- Warn leadership in the memo that the pipeline number will drop and that this is the desired outcome
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't recommend required fields on top of meaningless stage definitions - order matters
- Don't propose bulk deletes; archive with a backup and a documented restore path
- Don't design rules reps can only satisfy with junk values and expect real data
- Don't run any bulk CRM change from this memo - it authorizes a manager-reviewed dry run, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The recommendation traces to the named mechanism behind the symptom, backed by baseline counts
- [ ] Each option carries its political cost, not just its data benefit
- [ ] Safety rails (backup, archive, dry run, grace window) are conditions of proceeding
- [ ] The success measure is a checkable data condition with a review date
- [ ] No bulk change is executed by this skill; every mass update sits behind a human-approved dry run, and any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

```markdown

name: sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-operating-plan
description: >

metadata:
  category: sales
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for CRM hygiene: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "CRM hygiene operating-plan", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team is ready to execute a CRM cleanup and keep it clean: audit, stage definitions with exit criteria, zombie-deal purge, dedupe, field standards, and the ongoing cadence that stops the rot from returning. The one-time-blitz version of this project fails predictably - six months later the pipeline is fiction again - so this plan treats the maintenance cadence as a first-class workstream, not an afterthought.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- CRM platform, admin access owner, and the automations/integrations that touch opportunity, account, and contact records
- The audit baseline (or the queries to build it): zombies (no activity 60+ days), past-due close dates, missing key fields, duplicate estimate
- Current stage names and whether written exit criteria exist anywhere
- Who owns what: RevOps executor, sales managers (enforcement), rep communication owner, executive sponsor
- Reporting that depends on current (dirty) definitions - forecasts, dashboards, comp calculations

## Workflow

1. Run the audit as workstream one and publish the baseline numbers: zombie count and summed value, past-due close dates, missing-field rates, duplicate estimate. This is both the work queue and the before-picture that justifies the program when the pipeline number later shrinks.
2. Write stage definitions with verifiable exit criteria before touching any records - every downstream rule depends on stages meaning something. Exit criteria are observable facts, not feelings: "economic buyer identified by name," "next meeting scheduled with date," "written proposal delivered." Sales managers co-author them; criteria imposed on managers don't get enforced by managers.
3. Plan the zombie purge as a staged, reversible operation: (a) full backup/export, verified restorable; (b) dry-run list of candidates (no activity 60+ days, no next step, or close date long past) sent to each rep's manager; (c) a grace window - typically two weeks - for reps to rescue genuinely live deals by adding a real next step with a date; (d) bulk move to a closed-lost/archived state with a hygiene-purge reason code - never hard delete; (e) a summary of what moved, by rep, for the enforcement conversation. Every bulk step requires named-human approval before execution.
4. Schedule dedupe after the purge (no point merging records about to be archived): pick the surviving-record rule, merge in batches with spot-checks, and fix the intake problem - matching rules or ownership logic - that created the dupes, or they return.
5. Set field standards with the minimum viable required set: amount, close date, stage, next step with date. Every additional required field must name who consumes it and for what decision; fields nobody consumes become junk-value generators ("TBD", 1/1/2099). Validation rules land after stage definitions are trained, not before.
6. Before flipping any rule or bulk change, inventory the blast radius: automations that fire on stage change, dashboards and comp reports built on old definitions, integrations syncing the fields being changed. Test in sandbox where the platform allows; sequence changes so reporting owners are warned before their numbers move.
7. Stand up the maintenance cadence - the workstream that makes the rest stick: a weekly hygiene report to managers (new zombies, past-due dates, missing fields by rep), stage-exit criteria checked live in pipeline reviews, and a monthly metric review. Managers, not RevOps, deliver the accountability; the plan names which manager forum owns it.
8. Communicate throughout: what's changing, why (honest pipeline, fewer fire drills for reps too), dates, and the grace window. Reps hear it from their managers before any record they own moves.

## Output format

**Objective** - scope of the cleanup and the data condition that defines "clean"

**Baseline** - audit numbers as of the start date

**Operating plan**

| Workstream | Action | Owner | Deadline | Done means | Approval gate |

**Purge protocol** - backup, dry run, grace window, archive method, reason code

**Stage exit criteria** - the drafted criteria per stage, co-authored with managers

**Blast-radius inventory** - automations, reports, integrations affected, each with a warned owner

**Maintenance cadence** - report, forum, owner, frequency

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Verify the backup restores before any bulk operation, not after
- Archive with a reason code so purged pipeline is auditable and reversible
- Give reps a real grace window and route all purge lists through their managers
- Make every required field justify itself with a named consumer
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't hard-delete records - ever - in a hygiene program
- Don't run validation rules before stage definitions are agreed and trained
- Don't let RevOps be the enforcement face; managers own accountability in their own forums
- Don't execute any bulk change without a named human approving the dry-run list, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every workstream row has an owner, date, verifiable "done means," and (for bulk ops) an approval gate
- [ ] The purge protocol includes backup-verified, dry run, grace window, and archive-not-delete
- [ ] Stage exit criteria are observable facts co-owned by sales managers
- [ ] Blast-radius inventory covers automations, reports, and integrations with warned owners
- [ ] The maintenance cadence names its forum and owner; all bulk changes sit behind human approval, and any connector or high-stakes action requires sign-off
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown

name: sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-risk-review
description: >
  Review operational risk for CRM hygiene: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "CRM hygiene risk-review", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: sales
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-risk-review

Review operational risk for CRM hygiene: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "CRM hygiene risk-review", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this before a CRM cleanup or new hygiene rules go live - bulk archives, merges, validation rules, required fields, ownership changes - to catch the ways it goes wrong: real deals archived, automations misfiring on mass updates, comp and forecast reports breaking silently, reps gaming new rules, and leadership shocked by a pipeline number that suddenly tells the truth.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The planned changes, in order: what gets archived/merged/required/validated, affecting how many records
- Backup and rollback state: does a verified-restorable export exist? Is the purge archive-based or delete-based?
- The automation and integration inventory: workflows firing on stage/field changes, syncs (marketing platform, data warehouse, billing), comp calculations reading opportunity data
- How the dry run and rep grace window are designed, if at all
- Who has been told the pipeline number will shrink, and how forecasts currently consume the affected fields

## Workflow

1. Start with irreversibility: is any step a hard delete or an un-audited merge? Merges are effectively irreversible on most platforms - losing which record survived, and activity history with it. Verify a restorable backup exists (restore a sample, don't trust the export job's green checkmark) before anything else in the plan is even evaluated.
2. Assess false-positive risk in the purge criteria: long-cycle enterprise deals, deals waiting on customer-side procurement, and seasonal buyers all look like zombies to a "no activity 60 days" query. Check whether the criteria exempt these patterns, whether managers review the dry-run list, and whether reps get a grace window to rescue live deals. A purge that archives one real seven-figure deal costs more than the cleanup saved.
3. Trace the automation cascade: a bulk stage change can fire customer-facing emails, task creation, slack alerts, and sync updates at volume. Inventory every workflow triggered by the fields being touched; the mitigation is disabling or scoping automations during the bulk window - and any automation that emails customers is a hard blocker until confirmed off.
4. Trace reporting and money paths: forecasts, dashboards, and especially compensation calculations that read the affected fields or stages. A hygiene change that silently alters comp numbers becomes a trust incident; each affected report needs a named owner warned with a date, and comp-touching changes need finance sign-off.
5. Assess the human responses: reps satisfying new required fields with junk values ("TBD", 1/1/2099), reps parking deals in whatever stage avoids scrutiny, managers not enforcing exit criteria after week two. Mitigations are inspection points (junk-value reports, criteria checks in pipeline review), not more rules.
6. Assess the narrative risk: pipeline drops by the zombie value the day the purge lands. If leadership and the board-facing forecast owner haven't been pre-briefed that the drop is the removal of fiction, the program gets blamed for "losing pipeline." The mitigation is a before/after briefing scheduled ahead of the purge - check it exists.
7. Compile the risk table with likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner, and classify each as launch blocker (unverified backup, live customer-facing automations, comp path untraced) or monitored risk with a checkpoint date. Every bulk operation retains a named-human approval on its dry-run list.

## Output format

**Objective** - the planned hygiene changes and record volumes under review

**Inputs used**

**Risk table**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Owner | Blocker or monitored? |

**Launch blockers** - with the fix required and its owner

**Automation & integration trace** - each triggered workflow/sync, its bulk-window handling

**Reporting & comp trace** - each affected report, its owner, warned-by date, finance sign-off where money moves

**Purge false-positive check** - exempted patterns, dry-run reviewer, grace window

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Test-restore a sample from the backup before rating anything else acceptable
- Treat customer-facing automations firing during bulk updates as hard blockers
- Require finance sign-off on any change touching comp-relevant fields
- Schedule the leadership pre-brief on the shrinking pipeline number before the purge, not after
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't accept "the export ran" as backup verification
- Don't let purge criteria run without exemptions reviewed for long-cycle deal patterns
- Don't rate rep gaming as unlikely - design the inspection points that assume it
- Don't execute or approve any bulk operation yourself - dry-run lists go to named humans, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Backup restorability is verified (or listed as blocker #1)
- [ ] Every automation, sync, and report touched by the change is traced to a warned owner
- [ ] Purge criteria were checked against false-positive patterns with a manager-reviewed dry run
- [ ] The pipeline-drop pre-brief for leadership exists or is a named blocker
- [ ] Blockers are separated from monitored risks; all bulk operations keep human approval gates, and any connector or high-stakes action requires sign-off
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

4. Trace reporting and money paths: forecasts, dashboards, and especially compensation calculations that read the affected fields or stages. A hygiene change that silently alters comp numbers becomes a trust incident; each affected report needs a named owner warned with a date, and comp-touching changes need finance sign-off.
5. Assess the human responses: reps satisfying new required fields with junk values ("TBD", 1/1/2099), reps parking deals in whatever stage avoids scrutiny, managers not enforcing exit criteria after week two. Mitigations are inspection points (junk-value reports, criteria checks in pipeline review), not more rules.
6. Assess the narrative risk: pipeline drops by the zombie value the day the purge lands. If leadership and the board-facing forecast owner haven't been pre-briefed that the drop is the removal of fiction, the program gets blamed for "losing pipeline." The mitigation is a before/after briefing scheduled ahead of the purge - check it exists.
7. Compile the risk table with likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner, and classify each as launch blocker (unverified backup, live customer-facing automations, comp path untraced) or monitored risk with a checkpoint date. Every bulk operation retains a named-human approval on its dry-run list.

## Output format

**Objective** - the planned hygiene changes and record volumes under review

**Inputs used**

**Risk table**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Owner | Blocker or monitored? |

**Launch blockers** - with the fix required and its owner

**Automation & integration trace** - each triggered workflow/sync, its bulk-window handling

**Reporting & comp trace** - each affected report, its owner, warned-by date, finance sign-off where money moves

**Purge false-positive check** - exempted patterns, dry-run reviewer, grace window

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action**

## Do

- Test-restore a sample from the backup before rating anything else acceptable
- Treat customer-facing automations firing during bulk updates as hard blockers
- Require finance sign-off on any change touching comp-relevant fields
- Schedule the leadership pre-brief on the shrinking pipeline number before the purge, not after
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't accept "the export ran" as backup verification
- Don't let purge criteria run without exemptions reviewed for long-cycle deal patterns
- Don't rate rep gaming as unlikely - design the inspection points that assume it
- Don't execute or approve any bulk operation yourself - dry-run lists go to named humans, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Backup restorability is verified (or listed as blocker #1)
- [ ] Every automation, sync, and report touched by the change is traced to a warned owner
- [ ] Purge criteria were checked against false-positive patterns with a manager-reviewed dry run
- [ ] The pipeline-drop pre-brief for leadership exists or is a named blocker
- [ ] Blockers are separated from monitored risks; all bulk operations keep human approval gates, and any connector or high-stakes action requires sign-off

metadata:

---
# /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for CRM hygiene: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "CRM hygiene stakeholder-brief", /sales-revenue-ops-crm-hygiene-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a CRM cleanup needs stakeholders on side before, during, or after: leadership who must absorb a visibly smaller pipeline number, sales managers who must enforce new stage criteria in their own reviews, and reps who must change data habits. The most common failure of hygiene programs is communication, not queries - the number drops, nobody was warned, and the program is blamed for "losing pipeline" it merely stopped inventing.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Audience for this brief: exec leadership, sales managers, or the rep org - each needs a different version
- The program facts: what's changing (purge, stage criteria, required fields, dedupe), dates, and the grace window
- The numbers: current pipeline, zombie value to be archived, expected pipeline after - the honest before/after
- What each audience must do differently, and what enforcement lands in whose forum
- Which forecasts, dashboards, or comp reports will read differently after the change, and who owns each

## Workflow

1. Pick the version by audience and lead with what changes for them. Leadership: "pipeline will show roughly [X] lower on [date] because we're archiving deals with no activity in 60+ days - coverage was never real; forecast accuracy is the payoff." Managers: "you'll review a dry-run list by [date] and enforce stage criteria in your pipeline reviews from [date]." Reps: "deals with no next step get archived after [date]; here's how to rescue real ones."
2. Present the before/after honestly and in advance of the change landing. The pipeline drop must be pre-explained as removal of fiction, with the zombie value itemized (count, summed value, average age since last activity). A stakeholder who learns the number moved from a dashboard instead of this brief becomes an opponent of the program.
3. Explain the "what's in it for you" per audience without spin: leadership gets a forecast built on deals that exist; managers get pipeline reviews about real deals instead of archaeology; reps get fewer stale follow-up tasks and territory lists that reflect reality. Skip the corporate poetry - one concrete benefit each.
4. Lay out the timeline with the protective steps visible: backup taken, manager-reviewed dry run, rep grace window with rescue instructions, archive-not-delete with reason codes and a restore path. Stakeholders trust a plan that shows its safety rails; a brief that hides mechanics invites resistance.
5. Make the asks explicit and dated per audience: leadership acknowledges the number change before [date] (so the board narrative is prepared); managers return dry-run reviews by [date] and adopt the criteria check in their reviews; reps complete rescues within the grace window. One owner per ask.
6. Name what will be measured afterward (zombie count staying near zero, past-due close dates at zero, junk-value rates) and when the follow-up brief lands. Deliver this brief to the user as a draft - they review and send it through their own channels; enforcement messages come from managers, not from RevOps or from this skill.

## Output format

**Objective** - audience, the change, and the date it lands

**The number, before and after**

| Current pipeline | Zombie value archived | Expected after | Why this is an improvement |

**What changes for you** - per-audience actions and benefit, in their terms

**Timeline & safety rails** - backup, dry run, grace window, archive/restore path, key dates

**Asks**

| Audience | Ask | Owner | By date |

**What we'll measure after** - metrics and the follow-up date

**Risks / assumptions**

**Next action** - user reviews and distributes; manager-delivered messages noted as such

## Do

- Pre-explain the pipeline drop with itemized zombie value before it appears on any dashboard
- Write a separate version per audience - an exec brief pasted to reps reads as policing
- Show the safety rails (backup, dry run, grace window) prominently
- Route rep-facing enforcement through their managers' voices
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty

## Don't

- Don't soften the before/after numbers to make the program look gentler - surprise is the enemy
- Don't frame the change as "reps have been sloppy"; frame the system fix and the benefit
- Don't promise the CRM will "always be accurate" - promise the specific metrics and cadence
- Don't distribute this brief yourself or trigger any CRM change from it, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The pipeline before/after is stated in numbers, ahead of the change, with the reason
- [ ] Each audience version leads with what changes for them and one real benefit
- [ ] Safety rails and the grace window are visible in the timeline
- [ ] Every ask has an audience, owner, and date
- [ ] The brief is a draft for the user to review and distribute; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```
